mnemonics.title: Mnemonic 1
mnemonics.content: CLT: Can't Lose Track of the mean (the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes normal as n increases, regardless of the population's shape).

mnemonics.title: Mnemonic 2
mnemonics.content: Confidence Intervals: The Z's are for Known (use Z-distribution when population standard deviation is known), The T's are for Trouble (use T-distribution when population standard deviation is unknown and must be estimated).

mnemonics.title: Key Point 1
mnemonics.content: The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that the sampling distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normal for a large enough sample size (n ≥ 30 is a common rule of thumb), even if the original population is not normally distributed.

mnemonics.title: Key Point 2
mnemonics.content: When the population standard deviation (σ) is unknown (which is almost always the case), the sample standard deviation (s) is used as an estimate, and the Student's t-distribution must be used instead of the standard normal (Z) distribution to construct the confidence interval.

mnemonics.title: Key Point 3
mnemonics.content: The formula for a confidence interval for an unknown population mean (μ) is: Sample Mean ± (Critical Value) * (Standard Error), where the Standard Error is s/√n when using the t-distribution.
